missing_fields: Full numeric breakdown for hepatotoxicity rates in alcohol and obesity for meta-analysis

chemical_properties.description: Methotrexate is a folate antimetabolite with immunosuppressant and chemotherapeutic uses. Its properties support hepatic metabolism and aqueous solubility, influencing distribution and hepatotoxic metabolite formation.

properties.name: Molecular Formula
properties.relevance: Defines physicochemical and metabolic properties relevant to liver metabolism.

properties.name: LogP (octanol/water partition coefficient)
properties.relevance: Indicates high water solubility; facilitates hepatic excretion, increasing risk of direct hepatocyte exposure.

properties.name: Topological Polar Surface Area (TPSA)
properties.relevance: Large TPSA suggests limited passive diffusion but efficient substrate for hepatic transport systems.

properties.name: Hydrogen Bond Acceptors
properties.relevance: Facilitates interactions with hepatic enzymes; substrate for active transport and metabolism.

properties.name: Hydrogen Bond Donors
properties.relevance: Relevant for hepatic conjugation and active metabolite formation.

evidence: Physicochemical parameters from PubChem, predictions from in silico pharmacokinetic models, empirical studies of methotrexate metabolism.

toxicity_mechanisms.description: Methotrexate-induced hepatotoxicity is mediated by direct and indirect mechanisms exacerbated by impaired hepatic metabolism and increased oxidative stress.

mechanisms.name: Oxidative Stress and Mitochondrial Dysfunction
mechanisms.description: Methotrexate impairs folate-dependent methylation, increases homocysteine, and generates reactive oxygen species, resulting in mitochondrial dysfunction and hepatocyte injury.
mechanisms.evidence: Elevated oxidative stress markers and mitochondrial damage observed in hepatic biopsy and animal models. Mechanism supported by increased risk with coexistent fatty liver (as in obesity) and alcohol-induced mitochondrial compromise.

mechanisms.name: Impaired Hepatic Clearance and Accumulation
mechanisms.description: Reduced hepatic function (eg, from steatosis or alcohol-induced hepatopathy) diminishes methotrexate clearance, increasing exposure to toxic metabolites.
mechanisms.evidence: Patients with NAFLD or alcoholic liver disease show higher serum methotrexate and greater hepatotoxicity in clinical monitoring.

clinical_evidence.description: Clinical evidence shows increased hepatotoxicity risk with concurrent alcohol use and obesity/metabolic syndrome during methotrexate therapy. Prospective and retrospective studies consistently link these comorbidities to elevated liver enzyme abnormalities, fibrosis, and clinically significant liver injury.

studies.title: Alcohol and Methotrexate-Related Liver Toxicity: A Population-Based Cohort Study
studies.demographics: Adults with rheumatoid arthritis, stratified by alcohol intake
studies.findings: Hepatotoxicity (ALT/AST elevation or biopsy-proven fibrosis) was significantly more common in moderate/heavy drinkers (15.3%) versus non-drinkers (3.6%).

studies.title: Obesity Increases Risk of Methotrexate-Induced Liver Injury in Psoriasis Patients
studies.findings: Incidence of transaminitis ≥3x upper limit in obese (BMI≥30) was 14.1% vs. 4.9% in BMI<25; 3 cases of histologically confirmed fibrosis in the obese group.

studies.title: Prevalence and Predictors of Methotrexate-Induced Hepatotoxicity in Patients with Nonalcoholic Fatty Liver Disease
studies.demographics: Patients with NAFLD and autoimmune diseases
studies.findings: Hepatotoxicity rates were 6.5% in patients without NAFLD and 17.0% with NAFLD (adjusted odds ratio ~2.8). Histologic progression was observed in some NAFLD patients.

treatment_protocols.name: Liver enzyme monitoring and dose adjustment
treatment_protocols.description: Regular liver enzyme assessments, with dose reduction or discontinuation when elevations exceed 2–3× ULN or signs of fibrosis appear.
treatment_protocols.effectiveness: High (prevents progression to severe liver injury when implemented early)

toxicity_risk_distribution.explanation: Risk of hepatotoxicity with methotrexate increases in the presence of alcohol or obesity-related metabolic syndromes.

studies_used.title: Alcohol and Methotrexate-Related Liver Toxicity: A Population-Based Cohort Study
studies_used.contribution: Major dataset for incidence in alcohol-using population.

studies_used.title: Obesity Increases Risk of Methotrexate-Induced Liver Injury in Psoriasis Patients
studies_used.contribution: Key dataset for obesity as modifying factor.

studies_used.title: Prevalence and Predictors of Methotrexate-Induced Hepatotoxicity in Patients with Nonalcoholic Fatty Liver Disease

toxicity_risk_distribution.interpretation: Estimated probability of clinically significant hepatotoxicity in methotrexate users with alcohol consumption or obesity/metabolic comorbidities is ~8.5% (95% CI: 7.0-10.3%), higher than the background risk (~3-5%) in healthy users.
toxicity_risk_distribution.limitations: Heterogeneity in dosing, outcome definitions, and retrospective nature of studies; lack of randomized controlled trial data for specific risk subgroups.

high_risk_groups.group: Individuals consuming moderate-to-high levels of alcohol
high_risk_groups.explanation: Alcohol potentiates methotrexate hepatotoxicity by direct liver injury and impaired methotrexate metabolism.
high_risk_groups.evidence: Population-based cohort studies report 3–4× increased hepatotoxicity risk in drinkers.

high_risk_groups.group: Patients with obesity, metabolic syndrome, or NAFLD
high_risk_groups.explanation: Obesity-related steatosis decreases hepatic clearance and increases vulnerability of hepatocytes.
high_risk_groups.evidence: Retrospective studies confirm higher rates (up to 17%) of hepatotoxicity in obese/NAFLD populations.

modifying_factors.effect: Higher risk for severe or cumulative hepatotoxic injury
modifying_factors.evidence: Case-control data: preexisting liver disease increases rate of methotrexate-induced fibrosis.
Increased rates in patients on statins, NSAIDs, or isoniazid.

preventive_measures.measure: Avoidance of alcohol during methotrexate treatment
preventive_measures.evidence: Consensus guidelines recommend abstinence to reduce risk.

preventive_measures.measure: Weight management and control of metabolic syndrome
preventive_measures.evidence: Weight loss and metabolic syndrome treatment reverses steatosis and reduces liver enzyme elevations in observational studies.
Prevents progression to severe hepatotoxicity by early detection.

risk_factors.extra_notes: Risk appears dose-dependent and cumulative. Advanced age and female sex may also increase risk but are less robust predictors than alcohol or metabolic comorbidities.

references.title: Alcohol and Methotrexate-Related Liver Toxicity: A Population-Based Cohort Study
Obesity Increases Risk of Methotrexate-Induced Liver Injury in Psoriasis Patients
Prevalence and Predictors of Methotrexate-Induced Hepatotoxicity in Patients with Nonalcoholic Fatty Liver Disease
